summary: Gruber clarifies his earlier piece on Apple-Google tensions, emphasizing that the conflict goes beyond normal competition into outright hostility at the executive level. He draws on sources at both companies who describe the atmosphere as 'weird,' noting that rank-and-file engineers at neither company want or expect this fight. While engineers at each company have cultural critiques of the other, they generally like and respect each other. Gruber also corrects the record on Steve Jobs's town hall comments, noting Jobs specifically targeted 'teams at Google' — particularly Android — rather than Google as a whole. The piece frames the conflict as a generals' war being waged above the heads of the people doing the actual work.
key_points: The Apple-Google conflict has escalated from rivalry to outright hostility, driven by executives and upper management rather than engineers
Engineers at both companies find the hostile atmosphere 'weird' and are neither prepared for nor relishing the fight
Despite cultural differences, Apple and Google engineers generally like each other — many Apple engineers use Google services and many Google engineers use Apple hardware
Steve Jobs's fury was specifically directed at the Android team, not Google as a whole, a nuance lost in most reporting
Neither Apple nor Google has cultivated the 'us against the world' internal culture that old Microsoft had
money_quotes: I'm saying there's something else going on, something more vicious. Outright hostility. The difference between rival and enemy.
The word that keeps popping up regarding this situation is 'weird'.
Outright hostility just doesn't feel right to the engineers at either company.
Apple engineers tend to think Google makes ugly (but effective, smart) software, and they're suspicious of Google's 'we want all of your data' strategy.

Google engineers see that Apple doesn't get the web — or at least the web as a software platform — and consider that quaint, and they're flat-out offended by Apple's autocratic control of the App Store.

Neither company has cultivated an internal culture like, say, that of the Microsoft of old — an 'us against the world' mindset, where you know when you sign up for the gig that the goal isn't merely to succeed, but to destroy the competition.
This is a generals' war. The rank-and-file at Apple and Google aren't looking for battle, or even expecting it.
key_insight: The Apple-Google war is being waged by executives while the engineers at both companies — who generally respect each other — find the hostility bizarre and unwelcome.
